title: Why I'm doing a 30-day insurance AI challenge
hooks: I'm building 30 insurance AI apps in 30 days, end to end.
Not concept slides. Production-style tools for underwriting, claims, and operations.
body: The goal is simple: ship small, useful products that solve real market problems and can be demoed in minutes. This challenge is designed to turn AI from a slide-deck topic into repeatable operating value across the London Market.
cta: Follow the series if you want the build notes and lessons as each app ships.

title: How we are building it: the architecture behind the insurance AI monorepo
hooks: Every app starts with the same backbone: a shared platform, not a pile of one-off demos.
The point is to make 30 apps feel like one system, with clear boundaries and reusable parts.
body: We're building this as a monorepo with shared packages, app-specific schemas, and a consistent layered architecture. Data flows in a straight line: upload or API request -> validation -> repository -> service logic -> AI helper where needed -> response -> structured logs -> persistence in Supabase. That structure keeps each app demoable in minutes and scalable in code quality.
cta: If you were reviewing this architecture, where would you pressure-test it first?

title: How we are building it (the complete nerdy version)
hooks: This is the nerdy version: schemas, services, logs, AI routing, and test harnesses all wired together.
If you care about maintainability, the data flow is the product.
body: A request enters a Next.js API route, gets validated, and is routed to a deterministic service. The service calls typed repositories, optionally invokes AI extract/classify/summarise helpers, writes to app-specific Supabase schemas, and emits structured audit stages. Every app has requestId + appKey logs and explicit persistence status semantics: stored, skipped, failed.
cta: Comment “nerdy” if you want a per-layer walkthrough with example route + schema contracts.

title: What is coming next: grouped roadmap by insurance workflow
hooks: The next phase is not random app shipping. It's grouped by problem space and operating rhythm.
That way the series tells a coherent story instead of a scattered list of builds.
body:
We are sequencing by business value and workflow adjacency. The spine stays the same (capture -> extract -> decide -> explain -> audit), while the domain shifts by bucket priority.

Bucket priorities:
1. Lloyd's / Specialty Underwriting | Highest | 8 days | 27%
2. Exposure Management | High | 4 days | 13%
3. Claims Ops | High | 4 days | 13%
4. Broking / Placement | Medium | 4 days | 13%
5. Productivity & Automation | Medium | 5 days | 17%
6. Leadership / Team Ops | Lower | 2 days | 7%
7. General Insurance / Compliance | Lower | 3 days | 10%
cta: Which bucket should we accelerate first for your team?

title: Day 0 plan: build once, scale 30 times
hooks: Day 0 is not for features.
It's for building the foundation that makes 29 future apps faster.
body: The first step is a clean monorepo, shared packages, consistent testing, Supabase conventions, and a deployment pattern that works once and scales cleanly. That upfront work reduces friction and keeps quality consistent.
cta: If you are building at pace, start with the operating system, not the first feature.

title: Improvements done until Day 3
hooks: By Day 3, the value was no longer just in shipping.
It was in tightening the workflow around every build.
body: The first few apps exposed important guardrails: clearer extraction gates, cleaner schema permissions, stronger deployment patterns, and better handoff discipline. Each improvement reduced repeated mistakes in the next app.
cta: Every day should improve the next day. What guardrail gave your team the highest leverage?

title: Improvements done until Day 10
hooks: Ten days in, the stack had started to compound.
The apps were becoming more repeatable, more testable, and more useful.
body: The workflow moved from isolated prototypes to a repeatable delivery system: deterministic scoring, audit trails, shared UI patterns, predeploy checks, and cleaner Vercel conventions. This turns speed into something sustainable.
cta: If you want speed without chaos, standardise the hard parts early.

title: Overall lessons learnt until Day 10 and efficiency gains
hooks: The biggest lesson so far is simple.
Speed comes from standards, not shortcuts.
body: Shared foundations, explicit rules, and reusable patterns reduced setup time and improved consistency across underwriting and claims workflows. Efficiency gains came from fewer avoidable decisions, cleaner handoffs, and faster debugging.
cta: I'll keep sharing what worked, what broke, and what we standardised next.

title: Lloyd's/Specialty Underwriting - Section Start
hooks: Specialty underwriting wins before the quote is written.
The best teams make complexity look controlled.
body: In Lloyd's and specialty markets, edge comes from disciplined appetite, clear referral paths, and fast context.
cta: Where does your underwriting flow lose the most time today: intake, appetite checks, or referrals?

title: Exposure Management - Section Start
hooks: Exposure management is where assumptions get tested.
If you cannot see it, you cannot control it.
body: Exposure management is the control tower for accumulation, concentration, and steering decisions.
cta: Where is your biggest blind spot today: concentration by geography, class, or limit size?

title: Claims Ops - Section Start
hooks: Claims operations shape trust at the moment it matters most.
Speed matters. So does consistency.
body: Claims teams protect retention, brand, and capital through timely, documented, defensible decisions.
cta: What is the first claims handoff you would automate without reducing control?

title: Broking / Placement - Section Start
hooks: Placement is part market strategy, part execution discipline.
The right submission still needs the right market path.
body: Broking teams win by combining market knowledge with clean execution and sharper narratives.
cta: Where do placements stall most for your team: market mapping, wording quality, or referral loops?

title: Productivity & Automation - Section Start
hooks: Productivity gains come from removing friction, not adding dashboards.
Automation should free time for judgment work.
body: Best automation removes repetitive handoffs and improves data quality at intake.
cta: Which repetitive task should be removed first to release frontline capacity?

title: Leadership / Team Ops - Section Start
hooks: Team performance is built in the operating rhythm.
Leadership is mostly systems, not slogans.
body: High-performing teams share explicit priorities, visible ownership, and clear operating cadence.
cta: Which operating rhythm would improve outcomes fastest: clearer priorities, better handovers, or tighter feedback loops?

title: General Insurance / Compliance - Section Start
hooks: Compliance is not a side constraint. It is part of the product.
Best controls reduce risk without slowing business.
body: Governance works best when designed into daily workflows, not bolted on after.
cta: Which control is currently the most manual and could be designed into workflow?

title: Day 1: From broker submission to triage decision in minutes
hooks: Underwriters do not need more PDFs. They need a fast way to decide what deserves attention.
Day 1 turns messy broker submissions into a clear accept, refer, or decline recommendation.
body:
Problem: Submission packs arrive as inconsistent documents with missing fields and too much manual reading. That slows first-pass triage.

MVP achieved: Text-first intake that extracts key fields, scores appetite fit, returns a decision with reasoning, and stores results for auditability.

Scale path: Multi-document ingestion, broker workspace integration, class-specific rule libraries, and human-in-the-loop borderline queues.
cta: If you were building this for an underwriting team, what would you want surfaced first?

title: Day 2: Portfolio concentration risk should be visible before it becomes a loss
hooks: Good portfolios fail quietly when concentration builds in the wrong place.
Day 2 makes mix, territory, and limit pressure visible before the next bind.
body:
Problem: Static spreadsheets hide accumulation and class/territory skew until someone spots the pattern late.

MVP achieved: CSV workflow that analyses mix, flags concentration warnings, and adds plain-English commentary.

Scale path: Live monitoring, scenario overlays, threshold alerts, steering dashboards, syndicate governance reporting.
cta: Would you use this as daily monitoring or pre-bind decision aid?

title: Day 3: Risk appetite documents should be machine-readable
hooks: Most appetite statements are written for humans, but triage needs structure.
Day 3 converts policy text into actionable underwriting rules.
body:
Problem: Appetite guidance buried in prose makes consistent decisions hard.

MVP achieved: Parser converts statements into structured classes, territories, limits, exclusions, with Q&A lookup.

Scale path: Versioned appetite knowledge base with approvals, rule sync, and change tracking.
cta: How do you manage appetite drift today: manually or with a rules layer?

title: Day 4: Lloyd's slips need a faster first read
hooks: A slip can hide the issue in plain sight if every clause is manual.
Day 4 surfaces key terms, unusual clauses, and coverage gaps in one pass.
body:
Problem: Reviewers spend too much time extracting terms and too little on material clauses.

MVP achieved: Slip review flow extracts terms, flags unusual wording/gaps, and produces reviewer-ready output.

Scale path: Clause library comparisons, benchmark wording checks, endorsement awareness, specialist routing.
cta: What clause type should be auto-highlighted first in a real market review?

title: Day 5: Free-text risk descriptions should map to a clear class
hooks: When class coding is wrong, everything downstream gets harder.
Day 5 maps unstructured risk text to class-of-business with confidence.
body:
Problem: Vague descriptions are hard to categorise consistently.

MVP achieved: Classifier returns top class + alternatives, explains signal mix, warns on low confidence.

Scale path: Taxonomy normalisation, broker validation, DA checks, feedback loops for classifier quality.
cta: Would you trust this as underwriter assistant or front-door validation?

title: Day 6: Accumulation risk is easier to manage when you can see it
hooks: Spreadsheet totals do not reveal hotspots until they are crowded.
Day 6 turns location data into an accumulation heatmap with warnings.
body:
Problem: Teams need fast visibility of insured value clustering before crossing tolerance lines.

MVP achieved: CSV-driven heatmap validates locations, aggregates TIV, highlights hotspots, and adds commentary.

Scale path: Multi-peril overlays, cat scenario layering, near-real-time surveillance.
cta: What would make this production-ready first: validation, map fidelity, or scenarios?

title: Day 7: Cat event updates should become action, not noise
hooks: When an event breaks, teams need a briefing that says what changed and what to do.
Day 7 turns catastrophe bulletins into impact summaries and actions.
body:
Problem: Dense bulletins slow understanding of impacted classes and likely loss bands.

MVP achieved: Tool classifies event, estimates severity, identifies impacted classes, outputs concise actions.

Scale path: Multi-feed ingestion, timeline tracking, exposure overlays, escalation workflows.
cta: Should cat briefing optimise for speed, depth, or strict one-page operations?

title: Day 8: Endorsement changes can hide material risk
hooks: A renewal can look familiar until one clause changes the story.
Day 8 compares expiring vs renewal wording to surface material differences fast.
body:
Problem: Manual endorsement comparison misses subtle but material wording shifts.

MVP achieved: Diff checker flags material changes by severity with executive brief and audit trail.

Scale path: Wording intelligence, clause libraries, benchmark comparisons, specialist referral routing.
cta: Where would this save most time: underwriting, broking, or claims review?

title: Day 9: Referral queues need ranking, not just backlog
hooks: Not every referral deserves the same urgency.
Day 9 scores the queue so reviewers work highest-risk items first.
body:
Problem: Without consistent ranking, queues are driven by inbox order not risk/urgency.

MVP achieved: TSV scorer ranks referrals with weighted factors, critical warnings, and persisted analysis.

Scale path: Live queue orchestration, SLA tracking, team routing, capacity balancing.
cta: Would you replace queue order or use it as reviewer guidance first?

title: Day 10: FNOL triage should start with a decision, not an inbox
hooks: The first notice of loss is where speed matters most.
Day 10 classifies FNOLs into fast-track, manual-review, or escalate with factor-level reasoning.
body:
Problem: FNOL emails are messy and routing is inconsistent.

MVP achieved: Deterministic FNOL triage with reasoning factors and audit-friendly persistence.

Scale path: Inbox integrations, guided chat intake, policy lookups, fraud/severity signals, auto-routing.
cta: If you could automate one handoff tomorrow, which one is it?

title: Day 11: Binder capacity pressure should be visible before authority is breached
hooks: Binder drift is rarely one big event. It is small movements that compound quietly.
Day 11 monitors capacity utilisation and flags placement pressure before limits are crossed.
body:
Problem: Teams discover utilisation stress late when multiple placements consume authority at once.

MVP achieved: Capacity monitor calculates utilisation bands, warning levels, and referral-ready commentary with persistence and audit.

Scale path: Real-time bordereaux ingestion, broker views, threshold alerts, and automated authority controls.
cta: Which capacity threshold should trigger first action in your workflow?

title: Day 12: Treaty layers are easier to govern when loss flow is explicit
hooks: Reinsurance wording is clear only when someone can explain who pays at each band.
Day 12 parses treaty terms and shows deterministic payer flow across loss levels.
body:
Problem: Teams spend too much time manually interpreting attachment/limit interactions.

MVP achieved: Treaty parser extracts structure, validates required fields, and returns loss-band payer explanations with auditability.

Scale path: Multi-layer programmes, reinstatement cost modelling, and scenario overlays for placement and reserving teams.
cta: Would your team use this more for underwriting review or claims scenario validation?

title: Day 13: Schedule overlap should be flagged before accumulation surprises emerge
hooks: Two schedules can look independent while carrying the same risk concentration.
Day 13 detects cross-schedule clashes and prioritises them by severity.
body:
Problem: Overlaps across policy schedules are hard to spot manually at portfolio scale.

MVP achieved: Clash detector matches location/peril/period collisions and produces ranked clash alerts with persistence and audit trails.

Scale path: Network graph views, clash trend tracking, and automated referral orchestration across underwriting teams.
cta: Which clash dimension matters most for you: location, peril, period, or all three?

title: Day 14: Leakage signals should be surfaced before reserve drift becomes expensive
hooks: Claims leakage usually starts with weak early signals, not a single obvious error.
Day 14 scores claims rows for leakage pressure and governance follow-up.
body:
Problem: Late notifications, duplicate patterns, and reserve/paid drift are buried in large claims ledgers.

MVP achieved: Leakage flagger calculates deterministic risk scores, highlights drivers, and outputs reviewer-ready actions with audit logging.

Scale path: SIU integrations, workflow routing, recovery tracking, and continuous leakage surveillance.
cta: Which leakage signal would you prioritise first in your operation?

title: Day 15: Broker notes should become market-ready submission summaries in one pass
hooks: Placement speed improves when submission quality is consistent from the start.
Day 15 converts broker notes into structured market-ready summaries with referral cues.
body:
Problem: Long narrative notes create uneven submission quality and manual rework before market release.

MVP achieved: Submission builder extracts whitespace fields, referral triggers, and readiness board outputs with persistence and audit.

Scale path: Template governance, class-specific playbooks, and collaborative broker-underwriter iteration loops.
cta: What is your single biggest blocker in turning broker notes into release-ready submissions?

title: Day 16: Scenario views should show baseline and stress in one decision frame
hooks: Portfolio stress discussions fail when baseline and stressed views are split across tools.
Day 16 models baseline versus stress outcomes from the same exposure dataset.
body:
Problem: Teams cannot quickly compare concentration drift and peril uplifts under stress assumptions.

MVP achieved: Scenario modeller computes baseline/stressed loss metrics, concentration warnings, and query snippets with audit persistence.

Scale path: Configurable scenario libraries, portfolio segment drill-down, and automated stress reporting packs.
cta: Which stress assumption drives most board-level discussion in your book today?

title: Day 17: Market reform contract checks should be machine-speed and audit-friendly
hooks: MRC compliance reviews are high-value judgement work buried under repetitive checks.
Day 17 validates required fields and clause signals with deterministic outputs.
body:
Problem: Manual MRC checks are time-consuming and inconsistent across reviewers.

MVP achieved: MRC checker runs required-field gates, clause checks, warnings, and full whitespace extraction status with persisted audit.

Scale path: Clause benchmark libraries, broker feedback loops, and automated exception routing to specialist reviewers.
cta: Which MRC check consumes most reviewer effort in your current process?

title: Day 18: Placement progress should be visible as a governed operating board
hooks: Placement teams need one source of truth, not status updates scattered across channels.
Day 18 tracks market responses, bind pressure, and next actions in a deterministic flow.
body:
Problem: Progress tracking across markets is fragmented, making escalation timing inconsistent.

MVP achieved: Placement tracker structures status, market actions, and governance cues with stored outputs and audit stages.

Scale path: Market API integrations, team workflow handoffs, SLA monitoring, and leadership-level placement dashboards.
cta: Would this help more in day-to-day broking execution or placement governance reviews?

title: Day 19: Wording risk needs a severity-led diff, not a redline wall of text
hooks: Teams do not need every wording change. They need materiality-first decisions.
Day 19 scores wording deltas by risk severity and highlights actionable differences.
body:
Problem: Traditional diff reviews create noise and bury material changes in volume.

MVP achieved: Risk diff checker classifies wording changes by severity, surfaces critical snippets, and persists analysis plus audit trail.

Scale path: Clause ontology, historical precedent matching, and automatic routing for legal/underwriting review.
cta: If you had one severity threshold for auto-escalation, where would you set it?

title: Lloyd's/Specialty Underwriting - Section Wrap
hooks: Strong specialty underwriting is built on repeatable judgment.
That only works when process supports people.
body: Best-performing teams balance appetite, speed, and consistency with clear control boundaries.
cta: If you improved one lever this quarter, would it be appetite clarity, referral speed, or wording quality?

title: Exposure Management - Section Wrap
hooks: Exposure management turns uncertainty into action.
Goal is not perfect data. Goal is better decisions.
body: Shared accumulation visibility helps leaders decide earlier and avoid surprises later.
cta: Which metric should drive weekly action first: top hotspots, accumulation trend, or tail exposure?

title: Claims Ops - Section Wrap
hooks: Claims operations are where process meets promise.
If the model is weak, customers feel it immediately.
body: Fast, consistent handling depends on ownership, documentation, and escalation clarity.
cta: What should be your lead KPI here: cycle time, leakage reduction, or consistency of triage outcomes?

title: Broking / Placement - Section Wrap
hooks: Placement is won in details before market sees the risk.
Better preparation improves both speed and outcomes.
body: Operational discipline plus market intelligence drives better placement execution.
cta: What would lift placement outcomes more right now: better risk narratives or cleaner market execution?

title: Productivity & Automation - Section Wrap
hooks: Automation should create capacity, not convenience.
Right systems make good habits easier to keep.
body: The biggest gains come from removing low-value manual work and standardising frequent rework points.
cta: Which workflow should be standardised next to remove avoidable rework?

title: Leadership / Team Ops - Section Wrap
hooks: Leadership is visible in team behaviour when no one is watching.
Consistency beats intensity over the long run.
body: Performance improves when priorities are explicit and accountability is routine.
cta: What one operating change would raise team consistency over the next 90 days?

title: General Insurance / Compliance - Section Wrap
hooks: Compliance works best when built into work.
Rules alone do not reduce risk. Good design does.
body: Practical and proportionate controls reduce friction while strengthening assurance.
cta: How would you simplify assurance while keeping controls proportionate and effective?
